Rolloff Processor Documentation

Overview

The Rolloff Processor is a spectrum shaper that applies brightness curves to harmonic content. It controls how quickly higher harmonics are attenuated using a power-law formula, with independent control over low and high frequency regions.

This is often called the “magic ingredient” that prevents static sound - rolloff is what makes different harmonic configurations sound musically distinct.

Category

Shaping (Orange) - Transforms spectral character

Core Concept

output_amplitude[h] = input_amplitude[h] / h^rolloff

Where h is the harmonic number (1, 2, 3, …) and rolloff is the power. Higher rolloff = darker sound (more attenuation of upper harmonics).

The dual-band system allows different rolloff powers for low and high harmonics, with smooth blending in between.

Ports

Input Ports

Port Data Type Default Description
spectrumIn Spectrum (empty) Harmonic amplitudes from upstream
lowRolloff Control 0.3 Rolloff power for harmonics below crossover
highRolloff Control 1.0 Rolloff power for harmonics above crossover
crossover Control 8 Harmonic number where rolloff transitions
transition Control 4 Width of blend zone (in harmonics)

Output Ports

Port Data Type Description
spectrumOut Spectrum Modified harmonic amplitudes

Parameters

Parameter Type Default Description
lowRolloff Float 0.3 Rolloff power for lower harmonics
highRolloff Float 1.0 Rolloff power for upper harmonics
crossover Integer 8 Harmonic number at transition center
transition Integer 4 Number of harmonics to blend across

Rolloff Power Effects

Rolloff Sound Character Attenuation Rate
0.0 Very bright No attenuation (all harmonics equal)
0.3 Bright Gentle roll-off
0.6 Balanced Moderate decay
1.0 Warm -6 dB/octave equivalent
1.5 Soft Strong attenuation
2.0+ Dark Very strong attenuation

Example: Harmonic Attenuation at Different Powers

For the 8th harmonic (h=8): | Rolloff | Multiplier | dB | |———|————|—–| | 0.0 | 1.000 | 0 dB | | 0.5 | 0.354 | -9 dB | | 1.0 | 0.125 | -18 dB | | 1.5 | 0.044 | -27 dB | | 2.0 | 0.016 | -36 dB |

Dual-Band System

Low Band (harmonics ≤ crossover - transition/2)

Uses lowRolloff power. Typically set lower (brighter) to preserve fundamental character.

Transition Zone

Harmonics in this range use cosine-interpolated blend between lowRolloff and highRolloff. Provides smooth, click-free spectral evolution.

High Band (harmonics ≥ crossover + transition/2)

Uses highRolloff power. Typically set higher (darker) to control brightness and harshness.

Transition Calculation

transitionStart = crossover - transition/2
transitionEnd = crossover + transition/2

// In transition zone:
t = (harmonicNum - transitionStart) / (transitionEnd - transitionStart)
blend = 0.5 × (1.0 - cos(t × π))  // Cosine interpolation
effectiveRolloff = lowRolloff + (highRolloff - lowRolloff) × blend

Usage Patterns

Basic Spectrum Shaping

Harmonic Generator ──→ spectrumIn ──→ Rolloff Processor ──→ spectrumOut ──→ Spectrum to Signal

Brightness Modulation

Connect an envelope or LFO to animate brightness over time:

Envelope Engine ──→ lowRolloff
                          │
Harmonic Generator ──→ Rolloff Processor ──→ Spectrum to Signal

Multi-Stage Shaping

Chain multiple Rolloff Processors for complex curves:

Harmonic Generator ──→ Rolloff 1 (overall shape) ──→ Rolloff 2 (fine-tune highs) ──→ Spectrum to Signal

Preset Suggestions

Instrument Characters

Instrument lowRolloff highRolloff crossover transition
Bright Brass 0.2 0.8 6 4
Warm Strings 0.4 1.5 8 4
Soft Woodwind 0.3 1.2 5 3
Percussive 0.1 2.0 4 2
Dark Pad 0.6 2.0 6 6

Dynamic Expression

Phase lowRolloff highRolloff Character
Attack 0.1 0.5 Bright, cutting
Sustain 0.3 1.0 Balanced
Release 0.5 1.5 Softening

Important Behaviors

Multiplicative Nature

Rolloff only attenuates - it never adds harmonics or boosts amplitude. Output amplitudes are always ≤ input amplitudes.

Crossover Clamping

Crossover is clamped to minimum of 1 (can’t be below the fundamental).

Transition Width 0

Setting transition to 0 creates an abrupt change at the crossover point (no blending).

Inverted Rolloff

Setting lowRolloff > highRolloff is valid and creates unusual timbres where upper harmonics are relatively louder than lower ones (within the rolloff curve).

Comparison with Other Shapers

Container Domain Effect
Rolloff Processor Spectrum Power-law harmonic attenuation
Formant Body Signal Resonant filtering at formant frequencies
Noise Color Filter Signal Frequency-dependent noise shaping

Rolloff Processor works in the spectrum domain (before conversion to audio), while Formant Body and filters work on the audio signal itself.

Technical Notes

Mathematical Foundation

The rolloff formula 1/h^p is equivalent to: - p = 1.0: -6 dB per octave (natural harmonic decay) - p = 2.0: -12 dB per octave - p = 0.5: -3 dB per octave

Cosine Interpolation

The transition zone uses cosine interpolation which provides: - C0 continuity (no amplitude jumps) - C1 continuity (no derivative discontinuities) - Smooth perceptual transition

Fundamental Handling

The fundamental (h=1) is always divided by 1^p = 1, so it passes through unchanged regardless of rolloff power.

Documentation accurate as of Kala v1.4. Source files: rolloffprocessor.h, rolloffprocessor.cpp
